text: During the laparoscopic myomectomy, multiple myomas are visible, with three located on the anterior uterine wall. Two of these are large, and one is small. On the posterior wall, a large myoma is present on the right side. The surgeon injects vasopressin at the base of the anterior myoma.
The vasopressin injection causes transient ischemia, visibly changing the color of the myoma on the anterior wall.
The surgeon uses a myoma screw to hold the anterior myoma, providing anterior traction, while a harmonic scalpel is used to excise the myoma.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut and separate a sub-serous myoma, which is visually easier to remove due to its location beneath the serosa.
The surgeon deposits the removed myoma into the posterior cul-de-sac following its excision.
The surgeon excises another anterior myoma, using surgical instruments to carefully separate it from the surrounding uterine tissue.
The surgeon identifies a subserous myoma, which is positioned for removal using surgical instruments.
The myoma screw is used to lift the anterior myoma, providing traction for further excision.
The surgeon slowly enucleates the subserous myoma, carefully separating it from surrounding tissue using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigation instrument, likely to clear the surgical field of fluids and debris.
The surgeon uses a blunt dissector to facilitate the enucleation of the anterior myoma, aiding in the separation of the myoma from surrounding tissue.
The myoma is deposited into the cul-de-sac, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the third myoma, preparing for its enucleation from the uterine tissue.
The surgeon observes a large myoma in the surgical field.
The surgeon focuses on the posterior wall of the uterus, moving towards the right side, possibly preparing for further dissection or manipulation.
Vasopressin is injected into the base of the myoma to reduce bleeding and facilitate enucleation.
The surgeon uses an inharmonic device to make a longitudinal incision in the myoma, minimizing damage to the surrounding uterine muscle tissue.
The surgeon excises the incision further, increasing its size on the posterior wall towards the right side.
The myoma screw is used to hold the myoma in place, providing stability for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon uses suction irrigation to clear the surgical field, removing fluids and debris to improve visibility during the procedure.
The surgeon enucleates the myoma by carefully dissecting along the correct anatomical plane, separating it from surrounding uterine tissue.
The surgeon observes the myoma, noting its soft texture, which may affect the ease of enucleation and manipulation during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully separates the capsule of the softer myoma, using suction irrigation to maintain a clear plane of dissection.
The surgeon uses a blunt dissector to manipulate the myoma, which is held in place by a myoma screw, facilitating the separation of the capsule from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses suction as a blunt disector to separate the capsule from the softer myoma, aiming to establish a clear plane of dissection.
The myoma appears white due to transient ischemia caused by the administration of vasopressin.
The surgeon prepares for bloodless enucleation of the myoma, utilizing techniques to minimize bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon achieves a good dissection plane and removes a slightly soft myoma from the uterine tissue.
The surgeon utilizes a supraumbilical port, which is visibly effective for accessing the surgical site.
The surgeon successfully removes the myoma, completing the excision process.
The surgeon begins placing a tension suture, likely to approximate the edges of the uterine incision and minimize muscle injury during the myomectomy procedure.
Two small myomas are visible on the posterior wall of the uterus.
The surgeon prepares to remove another myoma from the uterine wall.
The surgeon uses a hook instrument to remove a myoma from the uterine wall.
The surgeon uses the heel of the hook to cut the tissue, likely targeting a myoma on the uterine wall.
The surgeon removes a myoma from the posterior wall using a surgical instrument.
The surgeon identifies another myoma on the posterior wall, preparing to remove it using the surgical hook.
The surgeon uses a hook to remove another myoma from the uterine tissue.
The surgeon uses a hook to make a longitudinal incision on the surface of the tissue, aiming to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon carefully enucleates the fibroid, gradually separating it from the surrounding uterine tissue.
The surgeon identifies a small myoma located on the anterior wall near the ostea and begins the process of separating it from the surrounding uterine tissue.
The surgeon uses stone holding forceps to remove the myoma, which is visible being extracted from the surgical site.
The uterus is visibly free of myomas, and the surgeon performs suction and irrigation to clear the surgical field.
The surgeon begins suturing the uterine wall after the removal of the myoma, ensuring hemostasis and closure of the incision site.
The surgeon places all removed myomas on the posterior sac for later processing.
The surgeon prepares to place a mattress suture at the surgical site, using a needle and suture material to secure the tissue.
The surgeon navigates either side of the surgical field to obliterate the dead space, ensuring no gaps remain in the tissue layers.
The surgeon uses a 35 mm endoskeen needle with number one vicryl suture material.
The surgeon takes both layers of tissue with the needle, preparing to return the needle back through the tissue.

The surgeon uses a Clark knot pusher to apply an extracorporeal square knot, approximating the muscle and obliterating the dead space. The suture behaves like a tension suture, ensuring the space is closed effectively.
The surgeon cuts the suture to a longer length, allowing the assistant to assist in managing the suture during the procedure.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing using the Dundee jamming knot technique, followed by Aberdeen termination, initiating intra-corporeal suturing with a starter knot.
The surgeon passes the needle through the loop to initiate continuous suturing, aiming to approximate the margins of the tissue.
The surgeon completes multiple bites in the locking blanket suture, securing the suture at the current point with a locking technique.
The surgeon uses the initial suture to tie a square knot, followed by a surgence knot, which is then terminated to secure the tissue.
The surgeon completes the termination of the first dandy jamming knot, preparing to continue suturing to approximate the tissue margins.
The surgeon completes a surgeon's knot to terminate the suture, ensuring secure closure of the tissue.
The surgeon tightens the locking blanket suture, applying tension to control bleeding at the surgical site.
The surgeon forms a C-shaped loop with the suture and then cuts the suture short, completing the knotting sequence.
The surgeon performs an extracorporeal Western knot to secure the remaining structure.
The surgeon feeds the suture through the tissue, preparing for the next step in the knot-tying process.
The surgeon removes the suture from the surgical field after completing the knotting sequence.
The surgeon slides the suture to approximate the tissue edges, ensuring proper alignment and closure.
The surgeon approximates the serosal surfaces anteriorly to prevent bleeding, ensuring the tissue edges are closely aligned.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigation device to remove blood from the surgical field, clearing the area for better visibility.
Following thorough suction, the surgeon begins the process of morcellation, preparing to fragment and remove the myomas.
The surgeon begins the process of morcellation, using a morcellator to fragment and remove the myomas from the uterine cavity.
The surgeon uses a saw-shaped Indian morcellator to effectively fragment and remove the myomas.
The surgeon slowly morcellates the entire myoma, breaking it down into smaller pieces using the saw-shaped Indian morcellator.
The surgeon uses stone holding forceps to extract broken pieces of the myoma from the surgical site.
The surgeon uses stone holding forceps to remove small pieces of myoma particles that have been spread across the surgical field.
The surgeon closes the port site to prevent the occurrence of an incisional hernia following the completion of the morcellation process.
The surgeon uses suture passers to close the port site, ensuring proper closure to prevent potential complications such as incisional hernia.
The surgeon uses vicryl suture material to push the suture inside the tissue, catches it, pulls it out, and ties a knot to secure the closure.
